evals.name: security-nightmare
evals.prompt: Standard roast this authentication module for me
evals.expected_output: Should identify: hardcoded secret, localStorage token storage, admin role escalation via query param, no rate limiting, in-memory user store, no token expiration. Score should be 2-4 (dumpster fire to rare). Roasts should be funny but accurate.
assertions: Identifies the hardcoded JWT secret as a CRITICAL security issue
Calls out the admin escalation via query parameter (req.query.admin)
Mentions localStorage as insecure for token storage
Notes the lack of rate limiting on login
Notes the JWT has no expiration
Provides a fix for each finding
Score is between 0 and 5 (this code has critical issues)
Output follows the specified format with Verdict, Findings, Scoreboard, Final Words
Roasts are funny but not mean-spirited
Acknowledges the bcrypt usage as something done right

evals.name: decent-code
evals.expected_output: Should find some issues (SQL search without sanitization, debug=True, no auth, datetime.utcnow deprecation, delete without confirmation) but overall recognize it as competent code. Score should be 5-7. Roasts should acknowledge good patterns while finding real issues.
assertions: Identifies the SQL LIKE injection risk in search_todos
Calls out debug=True in production
Notes the lack of authentication
Acknowledges good patterns (WAL mode, proper error handling, RESTful design)
Score is between 5 and 8 (decent code with real but non-critical issues)
Output follows the specified format
Does not manufacture fake critical issues to be dramatic
Provides fixes for each finding

evals.name: spaghetti-monster
evals.expected_output: Should identify: god function, global mutable state, recursive retry with sleep, 10+ parameters, no type hints, string-based dispatch, unbounded recursion risk, MD5 usage. Score should be 1-3. This is the ultimate roast target.
assertions: Identifies the god function (proc does everything)
Calls out the global mutable state (data, cache, log, errs)
Notes the recursive retry pattern as dangerous (potential stack overflow)
Mentions the excessive parameter count
Identifies the string-based mode dispatch as unmaintainable
Score is between 0 and 4 (this is genuinely bad code)
Output follows the specified format with all sections
Roasts are creative and funny, not generic
Suggests breaking proc() into separate functions as the primary fix
The Final Words section exists and delivers a memorable closing roast

evals.name: panel-roast-security
evals.expected_output: Should spawn 4 specialist agents, merge findings, produce a roast with Panel Notes table showing which agent found what. Cross-confirmed findings should be highlighted. Should catch everything the standard roast catches plus deeper specialist-specific findings.
assertions: Output includes a Panel Notes table with all 4 agents listed
At least one finding is marked as cross-confirmed (found by multiple agents)
Security Auditor section shows findings in the Panel Notes
Identifies the hardcoded JWT secret
Identifies the admin escalation via query parameter
Total finding count is equal to or greater than the standard roast
Output follows the format with Verdict, Findings, Panel Notes, Final Words
Score is between 0 and 5

evals.name: skill-roast-weak
evals.expected_output: Should identify that the skill is extremely vague — no trigger specificity, no output format, no process, generic rules, no edge case handling. Score should be 1-3 (napkin sketch). Should use the skill-specific categories and output format.
assertions: Identifies the trigger as too vague ('When the user asks' matches almost anything)
Calls out the lack of a defined output format or template
Notes the missing review process or workflow steps
Criticizes the rules as too generic ('Be nice' is not actionable)
Notes poor eval-readiness (you can't write assertions for undefined output)
Score is between 1 and 4 (this is a napkin sketch, not a production skill)
Output uses the Skill Roast format with skill-specific categories in the Scoreboard
Suggestions are constructive and specific enough to actually improve the skill
The Scoreboard uses skill categories (Trigger, Instructions, Edge Cases, etc.) not code categories

evals.name: diff-roast-sql-injection
evals.expected_output: Should identify: searchUsers now has SQL injection (was parameterized, now string interpolation), sortBy is unsanitized ORDER BY injection, bulkDelete has SQL injection via ids.join, getUser fields parameter allows column injection, exportUsers returns all data with no auth check mentioned. The diff REMOVED parameterized queries and replaced with string interpolation — this is a regression. Score should be 0-3.
assertions: Identifies the searchUsers SQL injection — the diff REMOVED parameterized queries and replaced with string interpolation
Calls out the sortBy parameter as an ORDER BY injection vector
Identifies the bulkDelete SQL injection via unsanitized ids.join
Notes the getUser fields parameter allows column name injection
Recognizes this as a REGRESSION — the old code was safer than the new code
Score is between 0 and 4 (these changes introduce critical security vulnerabilities)
Output follows a diff-specific format (mentions files changed, additions/deletions)
Provides fixes that restore parameterized queries
Does NOT blame pre-existing code — focuses on what the diff changed
Offers Auto-Fix after the roast

evals.name: batter-battle-cache
evals.prompt: Batter battle these two cache implementations
evals.expected_output: Should compare cache_v1 (module-level globals, no max size, no LRU, shadows builtin set) vs cache_v2 (class-based, OrderedDict LRU, max size, hit/miss tracking, get_or_set). v2 should win on architecture, style, and features. v1 might win on simplicity. Score should reflect v2 as the clear winner.
assertions: Declares cache_v2 as the winner
Notes that cache_v1 shadows the builtin 'set' function
Identifies cache_v1's lack of max size as a memory leak risk
Credits cache_v2 for LRU eviction via OrderedDict
Credits cache_v2 for hit/miss tracking and hit_rate stats
Notes cache_v2's get_or_set as a useful pattern v1 lacks
Output includes round-by-round comparison across categories
Output includes a scorecard table with per-category scores
Identifies at least one thing cache_v1 does better or simpler than cache_v2
Includes a 'What the Loser Should Steal' section with actionable advice

evals.name: language-pack-snoop
evals.prompt: Standard roast this authentication module for me. Use the Snoop Dogg personality.
evals.expected_output: Should produce the same findings as the standard security-nightmare test (hardcoded secret, admin escalation, etc.) but delivered in Snoop Dogg's voice — laid-back, slang, 'nephew', 'fo real', etc. Technical accuracy must not suffer for the personality.
assertions: Roast lines use Snoop Dogg voice — slang, laid-back delivery, hip-hop references
Does NOT use chef/kitchen metaphors (that's the default personality)
Still identifies the hardcoded JWT secret as a CRITICAL issue
Still identifies the admin escalation via query parameter
Still provides concrete fixes for each finding
Technical severity ratings are accurate (not inflated or deflated for comedy)
Output follows the standard format (Verdict, Findings, Scoreboard, Final Words)
The personality is consistent throughout — not just the intro

evals.name: auto-fix-offer
evals.expected_output: After the standard roast, should offer Auto-Fix with the menu: All, Critical/High only, Pick, or Nah. The offer should mention how many fixable issues were found.
assertions: Output includes the Auto-Fix offer section after the roast
Auto-Fix section mentions the number of fixable issues found
Auto-Fix section includes the options: All, Critical/High only, Pick, Nah
The Auto-Fix offer appears AFTER the Final Words section, not before
The roast itself is complete and valid before the Auto-Fix offer
The wrench emoji (🔧) appears in the Auto-Fix section
